evaluation_inputs.system_description: A reference architecture designed to standardize interfaces between software components for various ground-based command and control systems. It serves as a generalized architecture and common infrastructure to facilitate 'plug-and-play' integration, reduce development costs, and support strategic reuse across multiple government and commercial organizations.

phase_1_discovery.initial_documentation_status: The reference architecture was partially documented. The team had developed high-level Interface Design Language (IDL) and completed approximately one-fourth of the IDL for the 12 identified sub-domains. The specification was not complete.
evaluator_inquiry_questions: What are the specific quality attribute goals for the system?
How does the architecture support reasoning about tradeoffs between competing attributes like performance and reliability?

hardware_view_description: Ground-based command and control hardware infrastructure
runtime_constraints: Interoperability across autonomous and directed components

questions: How does the system ensure semantic integration between components, not just syntactic connection?
What mechanisms exist to guarantee real-time performance across the distributed middleware?

raw_candidate_scenarios.description: A system integrator incorporates a new vendor component into the system.
The system must operate continuously despite a partial network failure.

business_goals: Enable system builders to integrate systems on time and within costs
Drive down the cost of command and control systems
Facilitate 'plug-and-play' integration regardless of component location
technical_constraints: Must use Common Object Request Broker Architecture (CORBA)
Focus on interface definition rather than direct component reuse

architectural_approaches.description: Mandated use of Common Object Request Broker Architecture (CORBA) as the middleware standard to enable syntactic and semantic integration between distributed, heterogeneous components in a ground-based command and control context.

addressed_attributes.rationale: CORBA provides standardized IDL and communication protocols to enable components from different vendors/locations to interact.
Focus on interface definition (IDL) decouples components, allowing easier replacement or addition of new vendor components ('plug-and-play').

children.refinement: New vendor component integrates within 48 hours using standard IDL
children.scenario_description: A system integrator incorporates a new vendor component into the system.

children.refinement: Critical command response time < 200ms under normal load
children.scenario_description: Operator issues a time-critical command to a remote sensor during a mission.

children.refinement: System maintains core functions (degraded mode) during partial network failure
children.scenario_description: The system must operate continuously despite a partial network failure.

children.refinement: Replace a legacy data processor with a new one within one week
children.scenario_description: A legacy data processing component is deprecated and needs replacement.

children.refinement: Unauthorized access attempts are logged and blocked without disrupting authorized operations
children.scenario_description: An external actor attempts to gain unauthorized access to the command channel.

scenarios.scenario_text: A system integrator incorporates a new vendor component into the system.
Operator issues a time-critical command to a remote sensor during a mission.
The system must operate continuously despite a partial network failure.
A legacy data processing component is deprecated and needs replacement.
An external actor attempts to gain unauthorized access to the command channel.

scenario_reference.text: A system integrator incorporates a new vendor component into the system.

analysis_records.stimulus: Need to integrate a new vendor component
analysis_records.response_measure: Integration within 48 hours using standard IDL
architectural_decisions: Mandated use of CORBA with IDL
analysis_records.reasoning: CORBA's IDL provides a language-neutral interface definition, theoretically enabling 'plug-and-play' integration. The 48-hour goal assumes the new component's interface is fully compliant with the existing IDL and the ORB implementations are compatible.

risks.description: Vendor's CORBA ORB implementation may have proprietary extensions or version incompatibilities that break interoperability.
risks.rationale: Even with standard IDL, different ORB vendors may have subtle differences in protocol support, QoS features, or marshaling, causing integration delays.

risks.description: The new component's semantic behavior may not match system expectations despite syntactic IDL compatibility.
risks.rationale: IDL defines syntax but not semantics (e.g., valid parameter ranges, stateful behavior). Misalignment can cause functional errors post-integration.

non_risks.description: Syntactic interface mismatch is mitigated by the mandated CORBA IDL standard.
non_risks.rationale: The IDL serves as a contract. If both parties adhere strictly, the basic communication plumbing is standardized.

scenario_reference.text: Operator issues a time-critical command to a remote sensor during a mission.

architectural_decisions: Use of CORBA for remote object invocation
analysis_records.reasoning: CORBA introduces overhead from marshaling/demarshaling, network traversal, and ORB processing. Achieving a hard 200ms deadline depends heavily on network latency, ORB implementation performance, and message size. Without real-time ORB extensions, non-deterministic garbage collection or thread scheduling can cause latency spikes.

risks.description: CORBA middleware overhead may violate the 200ms response time requirement under load or with large messages.
risks.rationale: Standard CORBA ORBs are not inherently real-time; marshaling, IIOP protocol processing, and location transparency add latency.

risks.description: Network congestion or latency between command center and remote sensor could dominate response time.
risks.rationale: CORBA does not control underlying network QoS. WAN links may have variable latency exceeding the budget.

non_risks.description: The distributed object model provides a clear, location-transparent path for the command invocation.
non_risks.rationale: The architectural pattern directly supports the remote operation stimulus.

scenario_reference.text: The system must operate continuously despite a partial network failure.

analysis_records.response_measure: Core functions remain in degraded mode
analysis_records.reasoning: CORBA itself does not provide inherent fault tolerance for network partitions. Availability depends on higher-level system design: redundancy of components, failover mechanisms, and possibly replicated CORBA naming/services. The ORB may treat network failures as remote exceptions, potentially crashing clients if not handled.

risks.description: Single points of failure in CORBA services (e.g., Naming Service, Event Service) can bring down dependent components during a network partition.
risks.rationale: Core CORBA services are often centralized. Their failure disrupts object discovery and communication.

risks.description: The system may not gracefully degrade if critical remote objects become unreachable due to network failure.
risks.rationale: Clients may block or fail if they cannot locate or invoke remote objects, unless explicit timeout and fallback logic is implemented.

non_risks.description: Distribution itself allows for geographic redundancy of components.
non_risks.rationale: The architectural style does not preclude deploying redundant instances across different network segments.

scenario_reference.text: A legacy data processor is deprecated and needs replacement.

analysis_records.stimulus: Need to replace a legacy component
analysis_records.reasoning: The IDL defines a clear interface contract. If the new processor implements the same IDL interface, it can theoretically be swapped in. The one-week timeline assumes the new component's internal logic can be developed/tested independently and that integration is primarily about deployment and configuration.

risks.description: Legacy component may have implicit dependencies or side-effects not captured in the IDL, causing integration issues.
risks.rationale: IDL captures functional interface but not all behavioral semantics or environmental assumptions.

non_risks.description: The IDL provides a stable, versioned interface that shields the rest of the system from the component's internal changes.
non_risks.rationale: This is a core benefit of the interface-based decoupling pattern.

sensitivity_points.parameter: IDL Interface Stability and Backward Compatibility

scenario_reference.text: An external actor attempts to gain unauthorized access to the command channel.

analysis_records.response_measure: Attempt logged and blocked without disrupting authorized ops
analysis_records.reasoning: Basic CORBA does not include security; it relies on the CORBA Security Service (CSS) or external mechanisms (e.g., TLS, firewalls). The ability to log and block without disruption depends on the performance and configuration of these security layers. If CSS is used, it may add significant overhead.

risks.description: CORBA IIOP traffic may be unencrypted by default, exposing commands to eavesdropping or tampering.
risks.rationale: Standard IIOP does not mandate encryption. Security is an add-on service.

risks.description: Security enforcement (authentication/authorization) could become a performance bottleneck or single point of failure.
risks.rationale: Centralized security services can be attacked or overloaded, affecting legitimate operations.

non_risks.description: The CORBA specification includes a Security Service specification for implementing security.
non_risks.rationale: A standardized framework exists to address security concerns, though its implementation is optional.
